content:
# Acts Chapter 25 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 25 (Appeal to Caesar)

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Sentence structure and word order | "Festus therefore, having arrived-into the province, after three days went-up to Jerusalem from Caesarea." | "Festus therefore, having arrived in the province, after three days went up to Jerusalem from Caesarea." | "Three days after arriving in the province, Festus traveled from Caesarea up to Jerusalem." | Adopted LOGOS wording. PNEUMA reorders the sentence freely; EMET's "arrived-into" is overly wooden. LOGOS preserves Greek word order with natural English. |
| 2 | Verb choice for presenting charges | "made-clear [charges] to him against Paul" | "brought charges before him against Paul" | "brought their charges against Paul before him" | Adopted LOGOS. "Brought charges" is standard English legal language; EMET's "made-clear" is too literal for ενεφανισαν; PNEUMA adds "their" (not in Greek). |
| 3 | Rendering of the request and ambush clause | "asking a favor against him, so-that he-might-send-for him to Jerusalem, making-ready an ambush to kill him along the way" | "asking a favor against him, that he would summon him to Jerusalem — plotting an ambush to kill him along the way" | "asking as a personal favor that he have Paul transferred to Jerusalem — all the while plotting to ambush and kill him along the way" | Adopted LOGOS structure. PNEUMA's "personal favor" and "all the while" are interpretive additions; EMET's hyphenated compounds are wooden. LOGOS captures both the request and the sinister subplot cleanly. |
| 4 | Opening conjunction and verb choices | "Then Festus answered [that] Paul is-being-kept in Caesarea, and that he-himself is-about to go-out in-quickness." | "Festus then answered that Paul was being kept in custody at Caesarea, and that he himself was about to depart shortly." | "But Festus replied that Paul was being held in Caesarea and that he himself would be returning there shortly." | Adopted "But Festus answered" (PNEUMA's "But" for δε; LOGOS's "answered" for απεκριθη). LOGOS adds "in custody" (implicit but helpful). PNEUMA's "returning" misreads the direction of travel. Used LOGOS as base with "But." |
| 5 | Structure and speech rendering | "Those therefore among you... the ones-having-power, having-gone-down-together, if there-is anything wrong in the man, let-them-accuse him." | "let those among you who are in authority come down with me, and if there is anything wrong with this man, let them bring charges against him." | "Let your leading men come down with me... and if this man has done anything wrong, they can press charges against him there." | Adopted LOGOS. Natural English while preserving the conditional structure. PNEUMA adds "there" (not in Greek) and "leading men" interprets δυνατοι too freely. EMET is overly literal. |
| 7 | Description of accusations | "bringing many and heavy charges" | "bringing many and serious charges" | "hurling one serious accusation after another" | Adopted LOGOS's "serious" for βαρεα (EMET's "heavy" is too literal for the metaphorical sense; PNEUMA's "hurling" is interpretive). |
| 8 | Framing of Paul's defense | "Paul making-defense [that]: 'Neither against the law...nor against the temple, nor against Caesar have-I sinned [in]-anything.'" | "while Paul said in his defense, 'Neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar have I sinned in anything.'" | "Paul made his defense: 'I have committed no offense whatsoever against the Jewish law, or against the temple, or against Caesar.'" | Adopted LOGOS framing. PNEUMA replaces "sinned" with "committed no offense," losing ημαρτον; EMET's participle construction is stilted in English. |
| 9 | "grant a favor" vs. "lay up a favor" vs. "do a political favor" | "wishing to-lay-up a favor with-the-Jews" | "wishing to grant the Jews a favor" | "wanting to do the Jews a political favor" | Adopted LOGOS. "Grant a favor" is natural English for χαριν καταθεσθαι. PNEUMA's "political" is an interpretive addition (though contextually accurate). EMET's "lay-up" is too literal. |
| 9 | Trial proposal wording | "Are-you-willing, having-gone-up to Jerusalem, there to-be-judged concerning these-things before me?" | "Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and there be judged before me concerning these things?" | "Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and stand trial before me on these charges there?" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "stand trial" and "charges" interpret rather than translate; EMET preserves Greek participle order but reads awkwardly. |
| 10 | Paul's assertion of standing before Caesar | "Upon the judgment-seat of-Caesar I-am-standing, where it-is-necessary [for] me to-be-judged. [Against] the Jews I-have-done-wrong [in]-nothing, as also you very-well know." | "I am standing before the judgment seat of Caesar, where I ought to be judged. To the Jews I have done no wrong, as you also very well know." | "I am standing right now before Caesar's tribunal — which is exactly where I ought to be tried. I have done nothing wrong against the Jews, as you yourself know perfectly well." | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "right now," "exactly," and "perfectly well" are emphatic additions beyond the Greek. EMET's word order is too Greek for English. |
| 11 | Phrasing of the appeal | "If therefore I-am-doing-wrong and have-done anything worthy of-death, I-do not refuse to-die; but if none of-the-things [is true] of-which these-ones accuse me, no-one is-able to-hand me-over to-them. I-appeal-to Caesar." | "If then I am a wrongdoer and have committed anything worthy of death, I do not refuse to die; but if none of the things are true of which these men accuse me, no one can hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar." | "If I am guilty — if I have done anything that deserves death — I do not refuse to die. But if none of their accusations hold up, no one has the right to hand me over to them as a favor. I appeal to Caesar!" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "as a favor" (interpretive, though echoing χαρισασθαι) and uses dramatic dashes/exclamation. EMET's hyphenated constructions are difficult to read. |
| 12 | Verb for consulting the council | "having-spoken-with the council" | "having conferred with his council" | "After conferring with his advisory council" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "advisory" (not in Greek). EMET's "spoken-with" is too literal for συλλαλησας. |
| 13 | Temporal phrase and participle rendering | "And days having-passed, some, Agrippa the king and Bernice arrived at Caesarea, having-greeted Festus." | "And when some days had passed, Agrippa the king and Bernice arrived at Caesarea, greeting Festus." | "Some days later, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived in Caesarea to pay their respects to Festus." | Adopted EMET/LOGOS "having greeted" (aorist participle ασπασαμενοι per Sinaiticus). PNEUMA's "to pay their respects" converts participle to purpose clause. LOGOS's "greeting" (present) slightly misses the aorist. Used "having greeted" with LOGOS's overall structure. |
| 14 | "more days" vs. "many days" vs. "several days" | "as they-were-spending more days there" | "as they were spending many days there" | "Since they were staying for several days" | Adopted LOGOS "many days" for πλειους ημερας. PNEUMA's "several" is acceptable but less precise; EMET's "more" is too literal for the comparative used as an intensive. |
| 14 | Introduction of the prisoner | "A-certain man is left-behind [as] a-prisoner by Felix" | "There is a certain man left behind as a prisoner by Felix" | "There is a man here... whom Felix left behind as a prisoner" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "here" and restructures the passive; EMET's present tense "is left-behind" is too literal. |
| 15 | Rendering of condemnation request | "asking condemnation against him" | "asking for a sentence of condemnation against him" | "demanded a guilty verdict" | Adopted LOGOS. "Sentence of condemnation" renders καταδικην precisely. PNEUMA's "demanded a guilty verdict" is too free ("demanded" is not in the Greek for αιτουμενοι). EMET omits "sentence of." |
| 16 | Legal custom rendering | "it-is not a-custom for-Romans to-hand-over any man before the-one-being-accused might-have the accusers face to face, and might-receive a-place of-defense concerning the charge." | "it is not the custom of Romans to hand over any man before the accused has met his accusers face to face and has been given opportunity to make his defense concerning the charge." | "it is not Roman practice to hand over any accused person before he has faced his accusers in person and been given a fair chance to defend himself against the charges." | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "fair" (not in Greek) and "in person" interprets κατα προσωπον loosely. EMET's subjunctive constructions are awkward in English. |
| 17 | "making no delay" vs. "I made no delay" vs. "I wasted no time" | "making no delay" | "I made no delay" | "I wasted no time" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "wasted no time" is colloquial; EMET's participial phrase is grammatically incomplete. |
| 18 | Verb tense for expectation | "of-evil-things which I was-supposing" | "of the evil things that I was expecting" | "any of the crimes I had been expecting" | Adopted LOGOS "was expecting." PNEUMA's "crimes" interprets πονηρων too narrowly; EMET's "supposing" is less accurate for υπενοουν. |
| 19 | "questions" vs. "disputes" | "certain questions concerning their-own religion they-had against him" | "they had certain disputes with him about their own religion" | "they had disputes with him about their own religion" | Adopted LOGOS "disputes" for ζητηματα. EMET's "questions" is too neutral; the word implies controverted points. PNEUMA agrees with LOGOS here. |
| 19 | Rendering of Paul's claim about Jesus | "concerning a-certain Jesus having-died, whom Paul was-asserting to-be-alive" | "about a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul affirmed to be alive" | "about a certain Jesus who had died — but whom Paul kept insisting was alive" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "kept insisting" adds durative aspect not in εφασκεν (imperfect, but not iterative emphasis); the dash is interpretive. EMET's participle chain is stilted. |
| 20 | "inquiry" vs. "investigate" vs. phrasing | "being-at-a-loss concerning the inquiry into these-things, was-saying whether he-might-wish to-go to Jerusalem" | "being at a loss as to how to investigate these matters, I asked whether he wished to go to Jerusalem" | "I was at a loss how to investigate such matters, so I asked whether he would be willing to go to Jerusalem" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "such matters" and "would be willing" add nuance beyond the Greek. EMET's "was-saying" for ελεγον is too literal; "asked" captures the force. |
| 21 | Rendering of "the Augustus" | "for the decision of-the Augustus" | "for the decision of the Augustus" | "for the decision of His Majesty the Emperor" | Adopted EMET/LOGOS "the Augustus." This preserves the Greek Σεβαστου as a transliterated title, maintaining the distinction from Καισαρ. PNEUMA's "His Majesty the Emperor" is dynamic but loses the specific titular reference. |
| 22 | Agrippa's expression of desire | "I-was-wishing also myself to-hear the man." | "I myself also was wishing to hear the man." | "I would like to hear this man myself." | Adopted LOGOS. The imperfect εβουλομην is correctly rendered as "was wishing" (polite/unfulfilled desire). PNEUMA's "would like" captures the politeness but loses the Greek tense. EMET's word order is awkward. |
| 23 | "display" vs. "pomp" vs. "pageantry" | "with much display" | "with great pomp" | "with great pageantry" | Adopted LOGOS "with great pomp" for μετα πολλης φαντασιας. All three are acceptable; "pomp" is the most traditional and precise English equivalent. |
| 23 | "commanders" vs. "military commanders" | "with both commanders and men of-prominence of-the-city" | "together with the commanders and the prominent men of the city" | "accompanied by the military commanders and the city's leading men" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "military" (implicit but not in χιλιαρχοις, which are specifically tribunes/commanders of a thousand); EMET's "men of prominence" is slightly awkward. |
| 24 | "this-one" vs. "this man" and address structure | "you-see this-one concerning whom all the multitude of-the-Jews petitioned me" | "you see this man about whom the whole multitude of the Jews petitioned me" | "you see this man. The entire Jewish community — both in Jerusalem and here — has petitioned me about him" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA breaks the sentence and substitutes "Jewish community" for "multitude of the Jews." EMET's "this-one" is too literal for τουτον. |
| 24 | "crying out" vs. "shouting" | "crying-out [that] he-ought not to-live any-longer" | "crying out that he ought not to live any longer" | "shouting that he should no longer be allowed to live" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "should no longer be allowed to live" adds "allowed" (not in Greek). All three preserve the essential meaning. |
| 25 | "perceived" vs. "found" | "But I myself perceived [that] he-had-done nothing worthy of-death" | "But I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death" | "But I found that he had done nothing deserving death" | Adopted LOGOS "found" for κατελαβομην. EMET's "perceived" is an acceptable alternative. PNEUMA's "deserving" slightly shifts from αξιον θανατου. |
| 25 | Second reference to Augustus | "this-one himself having-appealed to the Augustus, I-decided to-send [him]" | "when he himself appealed to the Augustus, I decided to send him" | "since he himself appealed to the Emperor, I decided to send him" | Adopted LOGOS. Sinaiticus reads Σεβαστον here, so "the Augustus" is correct. PNEUMA uses "Emperor," losing the specific title. |
| 26 | "anything certain to write" vs. "nothing definite to write" | "Concerning whom anything certain to-write to-the-lord I-do not have" | "Concerning whom I have nothing definite to write to my lord" | "Yet I have nothing definitive to write to His Majesty about him" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "His Majesty" replaces κυριω too freely; EMET's word order follows Greek too closely. LOGOS's "my lord" renders τω κυριω precisely. |
| 26 | "the examination having-taken-place" structure | "so-that the examination having-taken-place, I-might-have something to-write" | "so that after the examination has taken place, I may have something to write" | "so that after this examination I may have something to put in writing" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "put in writing" is slightly free; EMET's participial absolute is stiff in English. |
| 27 | Verb for communicating charges | "not also the charges against him to-signify" | "not also to indicate the charges against him" | "without specifying the charges against him" | Adopted LOGOS "indicate" for σημαναι. PNEUMA's "specifying" slightly over-translates; EMET's "signify" is archaic. |
| 27 | Sentence structure | "For unreasonable it-seems to-me, sending a-prisoner, not also the charges against him to-signify." | "For it seems unreasonable to me, in sending a prisoner, not also to indicate the charges against him." | "For it seems unreasonable to me to send a prisoner to Rome without specifying the charges against him." | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "to Rome" (not in Greek text). EMET follows Greek word order too closely. |

-- SOPHIA
